title: ADK-101: ADK Foundations
description: Master the basics of Google's Agent Development Kit (ADK). Setup your local environment, install the CLI, configure agent.yaml, and run your first agent.

highlights.title: CLI Installation
highlights.description: Learn to install the ADK command line interface across native systems and sandboxed containers.

highlights.title: YAML Configurations
highlights.description: Master the schema design of agent.yaml to register tools, parameters, and memory parameters.

highlights.title: Tool Authoring
highlights.description: Write custom tools using Python or TypeScript decorators with strict parameter typing schemas.

blocks.content: ADK Foundations: Introduction & Architecture

Welcome to ADK Foundations. Google's Agent Development Kit (ADK) is a modular, language-independent framework built to run autonomous AI agents in production. ADK structures agent loops, standardizes tool invocation formats, manages session memory boundaries, and facilitates integration with enterprise services.
Core Architectural Components
Before writing code, it is critical to understand the four primary pillars of the ADK system architecture:

items.title: Agent Engine
items.content: The central core that coordinates semantic routing, planning, and task execution sequences using LLM backends.

items.title: Tool Registry
items.content: A structured library of functions that the agent can execute, configured with exact JSON Schemas.

items.title: Workspace Boundary
items.content: A localized sandboxed folder structure restricting the agent's file system read, write, and execute permissions.

items.title: Session Store
items.content: A stateful ledger tracking model requests, response steps, token consumption, and variables.

blocks.content: Pre-requisites and Environment Check
Verify that your developer machine meets the minimum system dependencies before installing the SDK CLI tool:

items: Python 3.10 or higher (required for Python tool bindings and scripts)
Node.js 18.x or higher (required for TypeScript tool decoration and local web tooling)
Go 1.20 or higher (optional, required if building high-throughput native extensions)
Docker, Podman, or Distrobox (strongly recommended for sandboxed workspace containment)

blocks.title: Pro-Tip: Runtime Verification
blocks.content: Ensure Python and Node are in your system PATH by running 'python --version' and 'node --version' in your active shell prior to bootstrapping.
Check Your Understanding
Question 1: What is the primary role of the Session Store in the ADK architecture?

- A) It compiles custom Go binaries into OCI container images.
- B) It acts as a stateful ledger tracking conversational state, token counts, and execution traces.
- C) It is a firewall that blocks external HTTP traffic.
Feedback: Option B is correct. The Session Store tracks the step-by-step state of the agent run, including token usage and conversation memory.
Question 2: Which runtime engines are officially supported for writing custom ADK tool decorations?

- A) Python, TypeScript/Node.js, and Go.
- B) Only Ruby on Rails.
- C) Bash shell scripts exclusively.
Feedback: Option A is correct. ADK provides official language bindings and decorators for Python, Node.js/TypeScript, and Go.

modules.title: 2. Installing the CLI and Environment Setup

blocks.content: Installing the ADK CLI Tool
The ADK system is administered using the `adk` command line interface. This tool manages project bootstrapping, agent testing, local server execution, and production deployments.
Option A: Native Script Installation
For standard development workstations, install the CLI globally using the direct package managers or the secure bootstrap script:

blocks.code:
# Installing via NPM global package manager
npm install -g @google/adk-cli

# Alternative: Python package installation for script-heavy environments
pip install google-adk-cli

blocks.content: Option B: Sandboxed distrobox execution (Tier 3 Hardening)
For locked-down corporate workstations, run the installer inside a local OCI distrobox sandbox container. This avoids local workspace contamination:

blocks.code:
# Initialize a distrobox shell using the official ADK base image
distrobox create -n adk-box --image gcr.io/google-adk/adk-box:latest

# Enter the sandboxed shell to run the ADK toolchain
distrobox enter adk-box

blocks.content: Verifying the Installation
Run the diagnostics suite to confirm the CLI is authenticated and has correct file access permissions:

blocks.title: Diagnostic Logs
blocks.content: The 'adk doctor' command checks model connectivity, runs local permissions verification in ~/.adk/cache, and reports dependency versions.
Check Your Understanding
Question 1: What command should you run to verify that your system paths, Python models, and security dependencies are ready for ADK development?

- A) adk check
- B) adk doctor
- C) adk verify --all
Feedback: Option B is correct. 'adk doctor' is the official built-in CLI diagnostic suite.
Anatomy of agent.yaml

Every ADK agent is defined by a primary metadata and configuration file named `agent.yaml`. This file explicitly declares the agent's identity, the execution model, model hyperparameters, registered tool modules, and conversation memory windows.
Detailed agent.yaml Structure
Below is a complete, production-grade example of an `agent.yaml` definition file:

blocks.code:
version: "1.0.0"
agent_id: "document-analyzer-agent"
title: "Enterprise Document Analyzer"
description: "Extracts structured schema facts from uploaded financial reports."

model:
  provider: "google-vertex"
  name: "gemini-1.5-pro"
  parameters:
    temperature: 0.1
    top_p: 0.95
    max_output_tokens: 8192

memory:
  type: "sliding-window"
  window_size: 15
  compaction_policy: "summarize"

tools:
  - name: "read_file_content"
    source: "src/tools/file_ops.py"
    class: "ReadFileContentTool"
  - name: "regex_search"
    source: "src/tools/text_regex.py"
    class: "RegexSearchTool"

security:
  workspace_lock: true
  network_access: "restricted"
  allowed_domains:
    - "*.googleapis.com"

blocks.content: Key Parameters Explained

items: model.parameters.temperature: Determines token sampling randomness. In engineering tools, keep this low (0.0 to 0.2) to guarantee deterministic code outputs and strict schema adherence.

memory.compaction_policy: Defines how the agent compresses messages when context thresholds are exceeded. 'summarize' triggers a background call to summarize older messages, while 'truncate' deletes oldest turns.
security.workspace_lock: When set to true, restricts the agent from reading or writing files outside of its immediate execution folder.

blocks.content: Check Your Understanding
Question 1: Why should an agent.yaml used for backend data migration have a temperature parameter of 0.0 or 0.1?

- A) To reduce memory allocation size in the container.
- B) To ensure consistent, deterministic behavior and minimize hallucinated variables during parsing.
- C) To make the agent run twice as fast.
Feedback: Option B is correct. Lowering the temperature decreases model randomness, which is critical for structured data processing and code execution.

modules.title: 4. Lab - Creating Your First Custom Tool

blocks.content: Lab: Custom Tool Authoring
In this lab, you will author a custom file parsing tool in Python that validates CSV formatting. The agent will discover this tool via the `agent.yaml` registry and execute it during tasks.
Step 1: Write the Tool Source Code

Create a new file `src/tools/csv_validator.py` and write the Python tool utilizing ADK's decorator bindings. Make sure to define docstrings and type hints as the agent relies on them to generate the JSON Schema payload.
Step 2: Register the Tool in agent.yaml
Register the new tool file inside your `agent.yaml` file under the tools section:
Check Your Understanding
Question 1: How does the ADK runtime construct the JSON parameters schema that is shared with the LLM backend for tool discovery?

- A) It prompts the developer to author a separate JSON file manually.
- B) It parses the function signature type hints and docstrings of the decorated @tool function.
- C) It reads global environment variables.
Feedback: Option B is correct. ADK uses runtime reflection to inspect parameter types and descriptions directly from the code docstrings.
Running and Testing Agents Locally
Once your configurations and tool files are registered, run the local execution engine using the ADK CLI to verify agent outputs and validate tool interfaces.
Step 1: Execute with a Direct Prompt
Run the agent in single-prompt execution mode. This initializes the connection, registers your tools, passes the prompt, executes the loop, and prints output:

blocks.code: adk run --prompt "Validate if customer_records.csv contains headers: id, name, email. Use relative path: src/test/customer_records.csv"

blocks.content: Step 2: Run in Interactive Repl Mode
To test multi-step dialogues and evaluate memory window slide parameters, launch the interactive shell (REPL) interface:

blocks.title: Trace Output Folder
blocks.content: By default, every execution of 'adk run' dumps a step-by-step tracking log file into the workspace directory under `.adk/logs/runs/`. You can reference these files to diagnose tool execution arguments.
Congratulations!
You have completed ADK-101: ADK Foundations. You are now ready to advance to developer tooling and visualization dashboards in ADK-201.
